intro: Captives in the ancient Near East were the primary human spoil of warfare — defeated enemies who might be killed, enslaved, ransomed, or deported depending on the conqueror's policy. The practice of marching defeated peoples into exile was systematically employed by the Assyrians and Babylonians as a means of breaking national identity and preventing revolt (2 Kings 15:29; 24:14). In the law of Moses, Israelites were permitted to take female captives in war under specified conditions that protected their dignity (Deuteronomy 21:10–14). The New Testament transforms the imagery: Paul describes Christ as having "led captivity captive" at his ascension (Ephesians 4:8, citing Psalm 68:18), triumphantly leading the defeated powers as his own captives in a heavenly procession.
